AKU/AKHST 2026Scientific Conference · DSM
4–5 June 2026 · Dar es Salaam

AKU / AKHST Scientific Conference 2026

Leveraging Quality Health Care in Tanzania: From Evidence to Excellence

Serena Hotel, Dar es Salaam
4–5 June 2026
CPD Points · MCT Compliant
Workshops — Day 1

Hands-on pre-conference training · 4 June 2026 · Tap a workshop for details

🚑
From Panic to Prepared
Practical First Aid for First Responders · Room 1
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Hussein K. Manji & Dr. Elishah Premji
AudienceAll healthcare personnel, all cadres
RoomKivukoni 1
Content
1.Initial approach to an unresponsive victim
2.Primary survey in emergencies
3.CPR in adults, children and infants
4.Management of choking in adults, children and infants
5.Tips and tricks for first aid in common emergencies
💻
Future-Proofing Healthcare
Digital Innovation & Climate Resilience · Room 2
▼
🌍 Climate Change & Health Systems
FacilitatorGeorge Humba — GRI Certified Sustainability Professional
Content
1.Climate change and health systems: risks, pathways, and impacts
2.Assessing vulnerability and climate risks in health systems
3.Practical approaches to building climate-resilient health services
4.Sustainable and low-carbon health system interventions
5.Action planning and monitoring: integrating climate resilience into health system strengthening
🤖 From Data to Diagnosis — AI in Healthcare
FacilitatorDr. Deogratias Mzurikwao — ETH Lab, MUHAS
FormatInteractive — bring a laptop or tablet
Content
1.Why AI in Healthcare Now — and why it matters more in LMICs
2.How AI Actually Works — demystifying ML, deep learning, and neural networks
3.AI in Clinical Practice — live demos: echocardiography, Pap smear, TB screening
4.Ethical, regulatory & equity dimensions — Tanzania TMDA, data governance, algorithmic bias
RoomRuvu Conference Room
👶
Saving Small Lives
Essential Skills in Neonatal Resuscitation · Room 3
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Yasser Abdallah & Dr. Naomi Mwamanenge
AudienceAll healthcare personnel
RoomKivukoni 3
Content
1.Preparation for newborn delivery
2.The golden minute
3.Effective ventilation and chest compression
4.Post resuscitation care
🎓
Teaching Tomorrow's Health Workers
Teaching Skills for Medical Educators · Room 4
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Mariam Noorani & Dr. Nahida Walli
AudienceMedical educators and clinical trainers
RoomKivukoni 2
Content
1.Effective teaching in the clinical setting — wards, clinics and operating theatres
2.Teaching using simulation
3.Teaching large classes and small group sessions
4.Using Artificial Intelligence in teaching and learning
5.Designing effective assessments
📋
Beyond the Checklist
Practical Skills for Measuring Healthcare Quality · Room 5
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Amri Kyaruzi Ishengoma & Aminieli Usiri
AudienceHealth professionals, quality officers, clinical managers
RoomRufiji Conference Room
Content
1.Developing meaningful healthcare quality indicators
2.Sources of quality related data
3.Analyzing and interpreting healthcare quality data effectively
4.Utilising tools for quality improvement
5.The patient's perspective as a quality measure
💊
Making Medications Safer
Excellence in Medication Management Systems · Room 6
▼
FacilitatorsVaidehi Bhatia (Clinical Pharmacist) & Charity Alphonce (EHR Pharmacist)
AudiencePharmacists, nurses, clinicians, health informatics professionals
RoomRuaha Conference Room
Content
1.Quality manufacturing, governance & access to medications
2.Safe prescribing and administration
3.Medicine reconciliation and dispensing
4.Data and informatics in medication management
5.Pharmacovigilance and antimicrobial stewardship

← Abstracts & Presenters
43
Total presentations
36 Oral · 7 Poster
Day 2 · 5 June 2026
Theme 1: Quality Improvement and Patient Safety in Clinical Practice
10:30–11:45 · Hall 1 · Chair: Aminieli Usiri · Rapporteur: Dr. Daisy Mhando
Effectiveness of Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy compared to improvised materials and imported eye shield: An Experimental Study
Consolata Matee · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr. Satrumin Shirima, Dr. Gloria Mrosso, Mr. Omari Pinda, Dr. Evelyne Assenga
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Neonatal jaundice accounts for 21.99% of all neonatal admissions globally, with South-East Asia and Africa having high prevalence. Phototherapy remains the main treatment but is associated with retinal damage if eyes are inadequately shielded. Many low-income settings use improvised materials like gauze despite uncertainty regarding their effectiveness.
Objectives: To assess the effectiveness of the Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy and compare it with improvised materials (gauze) and imported eye shield.
Methods: An experimental comparative study measured irradiance transmission through three materials: gauze, an imported eye shield, and the Innovated Eye Shield. Testing used a double-sided LED phototherapy device at 15 cm.
Results: The Innovated Eye Shield completely blocked irradiance transmission (0 µW/cm²/nm), comparable to the imported eye shield. Gauze allowed partial light transmission (4 µW/cm²/nm lower sensor), demonstrating inferior protection.
Conclusion: The Innovated Eye Shield demonstrated effective protection comparable to imported eye shields and superior to gauze. Its reusability after sterilization makes it a cost-effective intervention for low-resource settings.
Risk Factors and Clinical Outcomes in Hospitalized Patients with Multi Drug Resistant Organism (MDRO) Infections admitted in a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Samina Somji · Doctor · AKU/AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance is rising globally, with Sub-Saharan Africa showing the highest mortality from MDRO infections. This study aimed to identify risk factors and clinical outcomes of MDRO infections at Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam.
Objectives: To identify risk factors and clinical outcomes of MDRO infections among hospitalized patients at Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam.
Methods: Retrospective analytical cross-sectional study of patients ≥18 years admitted between January 2021 and December 2023 with positive microbial cultures. Data extracted from laboratory records.
Results: 398 patients included; MDRO prevalence 62.5%. Commonest MDRO group: ESBL (52.8%); organism: E. coli (22.9%). Prolonged length of stay (OR=4.01) and genitourinary tract infection (OR=2.68) were strongly associated with MDRO infections.
Conclusion: High prevalence of MDRO infections identified. Stringent infection control practices and targeted interventions are important, requiring continued surveillance and robust antibiotic stewardship programs.
Enhancing Quality Improvement in IVF Practice Through Effective Quality Control in the Embryology Laboratory
Praveena · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Co-authors: Mr. Muralidhar Babu CV, Dr. Madhav Hirani, Dr. Sheila Karim R
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality control in the embryology laboratory is critical for successful IVF, as embryo development is highly sensitive to environmental and procedural conditions including incubator stability, culture media, air quality, and temperature regulation.
Objectives: To evaluate embryology laboratory performance as an indicator of quality control effectiveness using key developmental parameters across IVF cycles.
Methods: Retrospective observational study at Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre. 226 IVF cycles analyzed using routine embryology laboratory records. Standardized protocols followed throughout.
Results: 226 IVF cycles analyzed, 1,306 embryos formed. Fertilization rate 76%, cleavage rate 90%, blastocyst formation rate 70%, embryo utilisation rate 55%, indicating consistent developmental progression.
Conclusion: Consistent outcomes reflect stable laboratory performance under standardized conditions. Continuous monitoring and adherence to quality control measures are essential for reliable embryo development and sustained quality improvement.
Effectiveness of a Structured Handover Checklist in the Post-Anesthesia Care Unit on Reducing Information Loss and Complication Rates at MNH
Shivangi Mandania · Resident · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr. Respicious Boniface, Dr. Moses Mulungu, Dr. Mpoki Ulisubisya
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Effective communication during patient handover is critical for patient safety in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU). Unstructured handover practices are associated with information loss and increased risk of complications.
Objectives: To assess the effectiveness of a structured handover checklist in reducing information loss and complication rates at the PACU in Muhimbili National Hospital.
Methods: Prospective pre-post interventional study among 332 patients (166 per phase) at the Main Operating Theater. Baseline assessment, staff training, and post-implementation evaluation conducted.
Results: Good handovers increased from 10% to 81.3% (p<0.001). Complications decreased from 32% to 18% (p=0.004). Post-implementation patients had 48% lower odds of complications. Handover duration was not significantly increased.
Conclusion: A structured handover checklist significantly improved completeness and reduced complication rates without increasing duration. Standardized handover tools should be integrated into routine PACU practice.
Improving Quality of Clinicians Handover using the ISBAR tool in the Paediatric and Neonatal Units at a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Tatenda Magodi · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Clinical handovers are high-risk periods for patient safety that can result in delayed treatment, incorrect medications, adverse clinical events, and legal claims. An audit identified clinical handover as a contributing factor to below-benchmark performance and patient complaints.
Objectives: Using the PDSA framework: (a) assess current clinical handover practices (pre-intervention), (b) educate clinicians on the ISBAR handover checklist (intervention), (c) evaluate success of interventions (post-intervention).
Methods: The ISBAR tool was divided into 15 checklist questions with a Global rating score out of 5, assessed anonymously during bedside handovers. 50 pre-intervention and 50 post-intervention handovers assessed. Training involved a 30-minute lecture, videos, and group practice of case scenarios.
Results: Significant improvement in 7 out of 15 ISBAR parameters (p<0.05), and a 30% improvement in the overall Global rating score. Feedback from training was positive.
Conclusion: Formal training, structured approach, regular evaluation, and reminders improve quality of clinicians' handovers, reduce risk of adverse events, and improve confidence in giving and receiving handover.
Safe Phlebotomy Practice and Adherence to Infection Prevention and Control SOPs among Health Laboratory Practitioners in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Shekha Salim · Laboratory Technologist · MUHAS
Co-authors: Masatu Jonhas
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Safe phlebotomy is key for infection prevention in healthcare settings. Tanzania has established national IPC SOPs, but limited evidence exists on how well these are followed by health laboratory practitioners.
Objectives: To assess the level of adherence to safe phlebotomy practices and compliance among practitioners working in regional referral hospitals in Dar es Salaam.
Methods: Cross-sectional observational study in Amana, Mwananyamala, and Temeke hospitals. 303 phlebotomy procedures directly observed using a structured checklist adapted from Tanzania National IPC Standards.
Results: Overall adherence meeting the compliance threshold: only 8.6% of procedures. Hand hygiene performed before procedure in 19.5% and after in just 4.3%. Adherence significantly associated with professional designation (p=0.031) and IPC trainings attended (p=0.020).
Conclusion: Adherence to safe phlebotomy IPC SOPs was generally low. Regular refresher training, stronger supervision, and routine observational audits are needed to improve compliance and strengthen patient safety.
Theme 2: Patient-Centred Care, Access and Equity for Vulnerable Populations
10:30–11:45 · Hall 2 · Chair: Dr. Daudi Gideon · Rapporteur: Dr. Caren Mussa
Exploration of the Impact of Reconstructive Surgery on the Quality of Life of Women with Burn Related Deformities in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study
Nobert Kasinya · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Burn-related deformities are a major cause of long-term disability among women in low-resource settings. In Tanzania, ~40% of burns occur in domestic kitchen environments. Little is known about women's experience of recovery beyond surgical outcomes.
Objectives: To explore how reconstructive surgery influences quality of life of Tanzanian women with burn-related deformities, emphasizing functional recovery, psychological well-being, social reintegration, and autonomy.
Methods: Qualitative descriptive study at Aga Khan Hospital involving 10 women who underwent reconstructive surgery. Semi-structured in-depth interviews in Swahili or English, analyzed using Braun and Clarke's reflexive thematic analysis.
Results: Five major themes: functional restoration as pathway to regained autonomy; recovery as negotiated improvement; psychological reconstruction of self-perception; social reintegration; and environmental conditions shaping lived recovery.
Conclusion: Women interpreted surgical success primarily through restoration of function, autonomy, dignity, and social participation. Burn reconstruction services should integrate rehabilitation, psychosocial support, and structured long-term follow-up.
Early Breastfeeding Challenges and Associated Risk Factors among First-Time Mothers in Dar es Salaam & Pwani Regions of Tanzania
Neema Balige · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr Mariam Noorani, Dr Rehema Enock
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months is vital for child health, but many mother-infant pairs struggle with challenges including sore nipples, engorgement, mastitis, latching issues, and low milk supply.
Objectives: To assess the prevalence and factors associated with early breastfeeding challenges among first-time mothers in Dar es Salaam and Pwani, Tanzania.
Methods: Cross-sectional study with longitudinal follow-up of 383 first-time mothers. Data collected through interviews within 24 hours of birth and follow-ups at weeks 1, 4, and 6. Logistic regression identified associated factors.
Results: 45% of mothers experienced early breastfeeding challenges, mostly within the first week. Low self-efficacy (AOR 4.74) and lack of verbal advice on demand feeding (AOR 1.48) increased risk. Among those with challenges, 18% did not exclusively breastfeed.
Conclusion: Nearly half of first-time mothers face breastfeeding challenges early. Breastfeeding counselling during antenatal and postnatal care is essential, especially for those with low self-efficacy.
The Efficacy and Safety of Greater Occipital Nerve Block for Migraine in Pregnancy: A Systematic Review
Lusajo Mwagobele · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Migraine is common among women of reproductive age and may persist during pregnancy. Pharmacological treatment options are limited by fetal safety concerns, creating a need for effective non-systemic therapies.
Objectives: To systematically evaluate the efficacy and safety of greater occipital nerve block (GONB) for the treatment of migraine in pregnant women.
Methods: A systematic review per PRISMA 2020 guidelines. MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL, Scopus, Cochrane CENTRAL, and Google Scholar were searched. Risk of bias assessed using RoB-2 and ROBINS-I tools.
Results: Four studies (n=164 pregnant women) met inclusion criteria. GONB was associated with rapid pain reduction and decreased need for rescue medications. No serious maternal, obstetric, or neonatal adverse events were reported.
Conclusion: Available evidence suggests GONB may be an effective and safe option for migraine during pregnancy. However, evidence quality is limited (GRADE: very low to low), highlighting the need for well-designed prospective studies.
Exploring Quality of Life in Postoperative Rheumatic Heart Disease Patients at Jakaya Kikwete Cardiac Institute
Magreth Justine · Nurse · JKCI
Co-authors: Dr Loveluck Mwasha
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) causes significant cardiovascular morbidity in Tanzania, estimated at ~9% prevalence. Post-surgical evaluations have largely overlooked patients' long-term quality of life.
Objectives: To explore the lived experiences and perceptions of postoperative RHD patients at JKCI.
Methods: Qualitative descriptive study using purposive sampling. Semi-structured interviews conducted at JKCI, audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using thematic analysis.
Results: Patients reported significant symptom improvement but faced lifelong burdens of anticoagulation management, dietary restrictions, psychological distress, financial hardship, and fragmented continuity of care between specialists and primary providers.
Conclusion: Surgical success should be redefined to include patient-centred support. An integrated care model combining psychological support, financial assistance, and accessible post-operative services is essential.
Understanding Barriers Associated with Delays in Diagnosis and Treatment among Colorectal Cancer Patients Treated at a Tertiary-Level Care Hospital – Tanzania
Mike Kimambo · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Colorectal cancer causes disproportionate mortality in LMICs like Tanzania due to late presentation and limited access to care. Patient, provider, and system-level factors contribute to diagnostic and treatment delays.
Objectives: To understand perceived barriers associated with delays in diagnosis and treatment among colorectal cancer patients at a tertiary-level care hospital in Dar es Salaam.
Methods: Descriptive qualitative study at Aga Khan Hospital involving purposively selected CRC patients ≥18 years. In-depth interviews using semi-structured guide analyzed with thematic analysis.
Results: Nine participants included, most presenting at advanced stages. Six themes: fragmented referral pathways, poor health-seeking behavior, limited awareness of CRC, economic constraints, low provider capacity, and poor quality of life outcomes.
Conclusion: Delays in CRC diagnosis and treatment are multifactorial. Interventions needed include public education, provider training, improved referral systems, and financial protection strategies.
Clinical-Pathological Profile and Survival Outcomes of Pediatric Primary Brain Tumours: A Five-Year Tertiary Hospital Review in Tanzania
Chambega Chambega · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Martha Mkony, Hamisi Shabani, Lulu Chirande
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Paediatric brain tumours are the second most common malignancy in children. While survival rates reach 70% in high-income countries, fewer than 50% survive in sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting a significant disparity.
Objectives: To determine the clinical profile, treatment modalities, and survival outcomes of children with primary brain tumours at a tertiary hospital in Tanzania.
Methods: Retrospective review of 74 patients aged 0-18 years with histologically confirmed primary brain tumours managed between January 2020 and December 2024. Survival probabilities estimated using Kaplan-Meier analysis.
Results: Mean age 7.2 years at diagnosis. Most underwent surgery (88%). Overall survival at 1 and 2 years was 52% and 37.8%. Astrocytoma histology, gross total resection, and female sex were associated with better survival.
Conclusion: Paediatric brain tumour care in this setting is characterised by delayed presentation and low survival rates. Enhanced diagnostic capacity and comprehensive multidisciplinary paediatric oncology care are urgently needed.
Theme 3: Health Systems Strengthening and Workforce Development
10:30–11:45 · Hall 3 · Chair: Dr. Ritha Ako · Rapporteur: Dr. Edward Kijazi
Introducing Family Medicine in Tanzania: Strengthening Primary Health Care through the 5 Cs — Advocacy through a Deliberative Dialogue Symposium
Dr. Nancy Matillya · Family Physician · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Donatus Rutajama Mutasingwa, Enica Richard, Matilda Alfred, and 14 others
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Tanzania launched the Universal Health Insurance Act in January 2026. Achieving Universal Health Coverage requires a fit-for-purpose primary care workforce. Family physicians trained in the 5Cs are positioned to address fragmented care and rising NCD burden.
Objectives: To describe the design and outcomes of the first Family Medicine Symposium in Tanzania and reflect on the multinational academic collaboration that drove it.
Methods: Deliberative dialogue approach at the first Family Medicine Symposium, Morogoro, January 2026. 51 Tanzanian and international stakeholders from 11 countries engaged in facilitated discussions using the 5Cs framework.
Results: Participants identified health system gaps family physicians can address. Family physicians currently trained at AKU; MUHAS and KCMC developing MMed programmes. 100% agreed the symposium strengthened understanding of the 5Cs and UHC relevance.
Conclusion: Scaling up family medicine training anchored in the 5Cs will strengthen primary health care and advance UHC. A formal scheme of service is urgently needed to enable trained family physicians to practise to their full potential.
Perceptions of Clients on the Performance of Hospital Quality Improvement Teams at Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre
Maiko Malecela · Health Secretary (Intern) · Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality improvement in healthcare is a crucial strategy for enhancing patient outcomes and operational efficiency. In Tanzania, QI initiatives aim to strengthen health systems, but gaps remain in their implementation.
Objectives: To assess clients' perceptions on the performance of hospital Quality Improvement Teams at Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre.
Methods: Cross-sectional design recruiting 105 participants using purposive and convenience sampling. Questionnaire and interview methods; SPSS version 20 for quantitative analysis; thematic analysis for qualitative data.
Results: 79.2% were not familiar with QIT roles and functions. 64.3% showed dissatisfaction with QI initiatives. Challenges included financial constraints (62.3%), inadequate trained personnel (53.3%), and low client engagement (58.4%).
Conclusion: Emphasis should be placed on raising awareness of QIT roles, improving resource mobilization, and enhancing effective operation of quality improvement initiatives to improve healthcare quality.
Factors Influencing Medical Officer Interns' Decision to Discontinue Clinical Practice after Internship in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Nathaniel Jones · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: The transition from internship to independent clinical practice is vulnerable. Evidence suggests many interns consider discontinuing clinical practice upon completion, raising concerns about early-career workforce retention.
Objectives: To determine factors associated with medical officer interns' decisions to discontinue clinical practice after internship in Dar es Salaam.
Methods: Cross-sectional analytical census among medical officer interns in Dar es Salaam (n=334). Structured questionnaire assessing socio-demographic, work environment, and psychosocial factors including burnout and mentorship.
Results: 49.1% reported intention to discontinue clinical practice. Burnout (p=0.015) and reward (p=0.026) were significantly associated with discontinuation. 48.2% reported >80 hours weekly workload.
Conclusion: A substantial proportion of interns consider discontinuing clinical practice, reflecting a multifactorial retention challenge. Health systems should implement workload regulation, structured rewards, mentorship frameworks, and psychosocial support.
Benefits, Challenges, and the Way Forward: Medical Specialists' Perception on Family Physician Integration in Tanzania
Juvenalius Anselm · Doctor · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Family medicine provides comprehensive care integrating biomedical, behavioral, and social sciences. While established in some sub-Saharan countries, it remains new and unintegrated in Tanzania's public health system.
Objectives: To explore medical specialists' perceptions on integrating family physicians into the Tanzanian healthcare system, identifying benefits, challenges, and recommendations.
Methods: Descriptive qualitative study through in-depth interviews with ten medical specialists from Aga Khan Hospital and Muhimbili National Hospital. Thematic analysis was employed.
Results: Integration was widely supported; benefits include holistic primary care, prevention, cost-effectiveness, and early disease detection. Challenges include limited awareness, lack of a defined scheme of service, and few training institutions.
Conclusion: Most specialists positively perceived family physician integration as transformative for primary care. Advocacy, policy reforms, and expanded training programs are crucial for successful integration.
Management of Clinical Alarms for Patients' Safety
Salum Omary · Nurse · AKH
Co-authors: Aika Mongi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Clinical alarms alert healthcare workers to physiological deterioration, supporting timely intervention. JCIA reported 98 alarm events including 80 deaths. Clinical alarms were frequently ignored or improperly individualized.
Objectives: To assess clinical alarm management practices, identify factors contributing to alarm fatigue, and implement structured alarm management strategies to strengthen patient safety.
Methods: Quality improvement project at Aga Khan Hospital across critical care units (ER, ICU, Cath lab) and other inpatient units. Questionnaires assessed knowledge, attitudes, and practices; observational tool assessed nurses during shifts.
Results: Only 28% of staff responded promptly to clinical alarm notifications. Alarm fatigue, improper settings, equipment failures, inaudible devices, inadequate training, and non-actionable alarms were key barriers.
Conclusion: Alarm fatigue, improper settings, and inadequate training weaken patient safety. Structured alarm management including staff training, policy development, and equipment maintenance are essential to strengthen patient safety.
The Pattern of Admission, Clinical Characteristics, and Outcomes among Patients Admitted to the ICU of a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania: A 5-Year Retrospective Review
Nadeem Kassam · Doctor · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: ICU mortality remains high in developing countries. Sub-Saharan Africa faces a double burden of rising NCDs alongside infectious diseases. Limited Tanzanian studies report high ICU mortality rates.
Objectives: To determine the burden of ICU mortality and identify factors associated with mortality among critically ill patients in Tanzania.
Methods: Single-center retrospective study reviewing records of all medically admitted ICU patients at Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam, from October 2018 to April 2023 (717 patients).
Results: 17.9% of patients did not survive. Highest mortality in septic shock (29.3%). Advanced age (OR 1.02), >3 comorbidities (OR 2.50), inotropic support (OR 3.58), and mechanical ventilation (OR 9.11) associated with increased mortality.
Conclusion: ICU mortality was lower than similar Sub-Saharan African studies. Advanced age, multiple comorbidities, and organ support were associated with ICU mortality. Large multi-center studies are needed.
Theme 4: Digital Health and Innovation for Quality Improvement
11:45–13:00 · Hall 1 · Chair: Dr. Syed Hassan · Rapporteur: Dr. Ronak Mulji
The Effectiveness of a Contextualized mHealth Integrated System in Reporting Obstetric Violence Incidents (VOICE-TZ)
Theresia Masoi · PhD Student in Midwifery · MUHAS
The Kikohozi Classifier Project: Harnessing the Power of Cough Sounds for AI-Powered Diagnosis of Multiple Respiratory Diseases in Low-Income Africa
Kahabi Isangula · Researcher · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Respiratory diseases including TB, asthma, and COPD remain major challenges in Tanzania where diagnostic capacity is limited. AI offers new opportunities for scalable, non-invasive diagnostics.
Objectives: To develop the Kikohozi Classifier, an AI-powered tool identifying TB, asthma, and COPD from cough sounds in primary healthcare and community settings.
Methods: Establishing the Kikohozi-DB, Africa's first large-scale cough sound database, recruiting 30,000 participants and linking recordings to verified clinical diagnoses. Advanced AI/ML methods used for model training and validation.
Results: 3,800 cough sounds collected from 760 participants: TB (56%), asthma (35%), COPD (8%). Preliminary analyses indicate promising potential in differentiating major respiratory conditions from cough characteristics.
Conclusion: This project strengthens Tanzanian research capacity in AI/ML with training programs and local infrastructure to ensure a long-term AI-driven healthcare ecosystem.
Promoting Exclusive Breastfeeding through Online Community-Based Support Groups in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Cynthia Masuka · Nutritionist · JJ Breastfeeding Organization
Co-authors: Idda Katigula, Dr. Mariam Noorani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Urban Tanzania's exclusive breastfeeding rates fall short of global targets. Barriers include limited skilled lactation support, early workforce return, sociocultural norms, and health system gaps.
Objectives: To improve breastfeeding knowledge and practices through community-based peer breastfeeding support groups in Dar es Salaam.
Methods: 20 community champions from public facilities received 18-hour training. They launched facility-linked WhatsApp groups enrolling pregnant/breastfeeding mothers. Monitoring assessed reach, participation, EBF rates, and support continuity.
Results: 955 mothers enrolled. Post-8-month intervention, EBF reached 72.88%, exceeding Dar es Salaam's 60.5% average. Limited smartphone access posed challenges, but facility-based education boosted participation.
Conclusion: Champion-led digital peer groups effectively elevate exclusive breastfeeding. Hybrid online-facility models overcome access barriers; scaling via government systems advances national nutrition targets.
Role of Preventive Maintenance in Enhancing Patient Safety and Healthcare Quality
Godfred Nzumbi · Biomedical Technician · AKHST
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Biomedical equipment plays a critical role in diagnosis, treatment, and monitoring, making equipment reliability essential for quality healthcare delivery and patient safety.
Objectives: To assess the role of preventive maintenance in reducing equipment downtime, improving operational efficiency, and supporting continuity of clinical services.
Methods: Data reviewed from preventive maintenance schedules, maintenance records, service reports, and equipment performance logs managed by the biomedical engineering department.
Results: Structured preventive maintenance schedules and improved documentation contributed to increased equipment reliability, reduced downtime, and improved coordination between clinical users and biomedical engineering personnel.
Conclusion: Strengthening preventive maintenance programs is an important strategy for improving healthcare quality and patient safety. Investment in digital maintenance tracking tools and continuous technical capacity development is recommended.
Transforming Breast Imaging in Tanzania: From General Radiology to Sub-specialized Breast Imaging at Muhimbili National Hospital
Irene Mhalu · Doctor · MNH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Breast cancer is the leading cancer among women globally, with many Tanzanian patients presenting with advanced disease due to delayed diagnosis and limited access to specialized imaging.
Objectives: To document the transition of breast imaging services at MNH from general radiology to a sub-specialized breast imaging unit.
Methods: Based on institutional experience and service development within the Women Imaging Unit at MNH through review of clinical workflow, imaging services, and multidisciplinary breast cancer care practices.
Results: Since 2023, services expanded from 2D to 3D mammography/tomosynthesis and stereotactic biopsy. The unit has performed 434 screening mammograms, 1,697 diagnostic mammograms, 2,843 breast ultrasounds, and five ultrasound-guided biopsies weekly.
Conclusion: Establishment of sub-specialized breast imaging demonstrates the feasibility of dedicated breast imaging services in strengthening multidisciplinary breast cancer care within resource-limited settings.
Transforming Antimicrobial Stewardship through Electronic Health Record Integration: Post-Implementation Experience from Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam
Charity Chiwambo · Pharmacist · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a major threat to patient safety globally. Aga Khan Hospital's Antimicrobial Stewardship Programme (ASP), established in 2017, was strengthened through MEDITECH EHR implementation in 2025.
Objectives: To evaluate the impact of EHR implementation on antimicrobial stewardship performance, comparing KPIs before and after system deployment.
Methods: Retrospective post-implementation evaluation comparing ASP KPIs from Q3 2025 (pre-implementation) and Q1 2026 (post-implementation). Data extracted from EHR dashboards, pharmacy records, and microbiology reports.
Results: Rational antimicrobial use increased to 89.4%. Culture collection compliance improved to 91%. Surgical prophylaxis adherence reached 99%. Antibiotic timeout alerts and automated renewal controls reduced inappropriate antimicrobial exposure.
Conclusion: EHR integration significantly strengthens antimicrobial governance, improves prescribing quality, and enhances patient safety. This model provides a scalable framework for digital transformation of antimicrobial stewardship.
Theme 5: Quality and Continuity of Care Across the Life Course
11:45–13:00 · Hall 2 · Chair: Dr. Rahma Bakari · Rapporteur: Dr. Murtaza Lamuwalla
Extending Hospital Care into the Community: A Home-Based Model Improving Access, Continuity, and Patient Safety in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Said Shukuru · Nurse · AKH
Co-authors: Aika Mongi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Continuity of care following hospital discharge is challenged by mobility, cost, and access barriers. The H2H2H (Hospital-to-Home-to-Hospital) program was designed to extend healthcare delivery into patients' homes.
Objectives: To evaluate the effectiveness of a home-based care model in improving access, service utilization, patient safety, and continuity of care using defined quality indicators.
Methods: Retrospective quality improvement review of H2H2H program data for 2025 using a KPI dashboard. Indicators: patient enrolment, service utilization, adverse events, readmission rates, and patient satisfaction.
Results: 1,049 patients enrolled from 1,508 screened. Low adverse events (1 reported) and consistently low readmission rates (≤3 monthly). Patient satisfaction exceeded 95% in later periods.
Conclusion: Home-based care is safe and feasible for improving access and continuity. Strengthening patient engagement, expanding coverage, and improving feedback mechanisms can enhance uptake and equity.
Determinants of Self-Medication Practices for Cough and Cold among Caregivers of Children in Dar es Salaam — A Mixed Methods Study
Sheliza Thaver · Resident · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Dr Nahida Walli, Dr Mariam Noorani, Dr Neelam Ismail, Dr Maria Bulimba
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Self-medication is a significant global public health concern. Caregivers frequently administer medications to children without professional advice for cough and cold. These medications lack strong evidence and have been associated with adverse effects in young children.
Objectives: To explore determinants of self-medication practices among caregivers for cough and cold in children under five years; and determine proportion, type of medication, and common sources.
Methods: Mixed-methods study (concurrent embedded design) among caregivers of children under five at Aga Khan Health Service Clinics in Dar es Salaam. Analytic cross-sectional and qualitative descriptive design.
Results: 70.9% self-medicated; cough syrup most common (51.4%); pharmacies most common source (75.8%). Determinants: older caregiver age, being a mother, having ≥2 children. Qualitative themes: HCW influence, healthcare system barriers, perceived illness familiarity.
Conclusion: High prevalence of self-medication for cough and cold in children. Addressing this requires strengthening regulatory frameworks, community health education, evidence-based pharmacist dispensing, and clear telemedicine protocols.
From Culture to Cure: The Clinical Microbiology Laboratory as the Engine of Evidence-Based Quality Care and Antibiotic Stewardship at a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Jonathan Shogholo · Laboratory Scientist · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) disproportionately burdens LMICs including Tanzania. Hospital Antibiotic Stewardship Programs (ASPs) are primary strategies to optimize antimicrobial use, with clinical microbiology as their foundation.
Objectives: To demonstrate the impact of a clinical microbiology laboratory on antimicrobial stewardship, prescribing practices, and clinical decision-making.
Methods: Retrospective analysis of laboratory-driven ASP interventions, including automated diagnostic platforms, standardization of selective reporting, and annual development of facility-specific cumulative antibiograms.
Results: Automated pathogen identification and antibiotic susceptibility equipment (Vitek 2 Compact) reduced time to effective therapy. Cumulative antibiogram data improved appropriateness of empirical prescriptions.
Conclusion: The microbiology laboratory is a strategic partner in health system strengthening, functioning as an indispensable pillar of the hospital's ASP generating actionable evidence for prescribing decisions and AMR surveillance.
Co-Created Clinical Practice Guidelines and Recurring In-House Training to Improve Healthy Survival at Birth in Urban Tanzania: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster-Randomised Trial (PartoMa Birth)
Natasha Housseine · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Birth-related mortality disproportionately affects LMICs, increasingly concentrated in congested urban facilities. Clinical practice guidelines are central to strengthening timely, evidence-based care, but are often developed without regard for implementation contexts.
Objectives: To assess the effect on perinatal mortality of locally co-created, contextualised clinical practice guidelines implemented through quarterly in-house seminars in urban Tanzania.
Methods: Stepped-wedge cluster-randomised trial in five governmental high-volume maternity units in Dar es Salaam. 65,181 mother-baby pairs included (22,338 before, 42,843 after). Primary outcome: standardised risk of perinatal mortality.
Results: Perinatal mortality decreased from 21.25 to 11.05 per 1,000 births (RR 0.52, p=0.002). Stillbirths with positive foetal heart rate fell from 8.26 to 2.51 per 1,000 births (RR 0.30, p=0.008). 7-day neonatal mortality fell (RR 0.62, p=0.030).
Conclusion: Birth-related perinatal mortality reduced following co-created, contextualised guidelines and in-house training. Co-creating context-specific clinical support tools holds underexplored potential for maternal and neonatal health care improvement.
Factors Associated with Screen Time Use among Preschool Children in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Elisamia Ngowi · Doctor · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Maria Bulimba, Mariam Noorani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Excessive screen time in early childhood is associated with poor sleep, overweight, and developmental delays. WHO recommends no screen exposure for children under 2 and maximum 1 hour/day for ages 2-5. No national guidelines exist in Tanzania.
Objectives: To determine factors associated with screen time usage among preschool children in Dar es Salaam and compliance with WHO guidelines.
Methods: Analytical cross-sectional study among 499 children aged 2-5 years from preschools in Ilala District. Logistic regression identified factors associated with adherence to WHO guidelines.
Results: Only 8.6% of children adhered to WHO guidelines, with a mean daily screen time of four hours. Device availability and caregiver-related factors appeared influential in unadjusted analyses.
Conclusion: The majority (91.4%) exceeded WHO screen time guidelines. National guidelines, caregiver education, and school-based programs are needed to promote healthier screen use in early childhood.
Exploring Challenges and Opportunities for Integrating Carbetocin into Maternal Healthcare in Tanzania
Euphraim Massanja · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) remains a leading cause of maternal mortality in Tanzania. Although oxytocin is first-line, cold-chain storage limits its effectiveness. Heat-stable carbetocin offers a potential alternative.
Objectives: To explore challenges and opportunities for integrating carbetocin into Tanzania's maternal health system for PPH prevention and management.
Methods: Descriptive qualitative study using purposive sampling. Key informant interviews and focus group discussions with obstetricians, anesthesiologists, pharmacists, nurses, midwives, policymakers, and professional body representatives.
Results: Five themes emerged: carbetocin's clinical effectiveness and operational advantages; procurement and supply chain challenges; policy prioritization needs; evolving provider acceptance; and cost concerns offset by long-term savings.
Conclusion: Carbetocin is a promising intervention for PPH prevention in Tanzania. Addressing financial, policy, and implementation barriers may support its integration into national maternal health strategies.
Theme 6: Quality of Research and Teaching in Health Care
11:45–13:00 · Hall 3 · Chair: Dr. Amri Kyaruzi · Rapporteur: Dr. Zeenat Juneja
Strengthening Evidence-Based Healthcare Through Research Capacity Building: Experience from Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training, Dar es Salaam
Khadija Abdallah · Researcher · Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training
Co-authors: Jackline Zambi, Magreth Angelus, Berthania Magesa, Kinari Swali
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Healthcare institutions generate large amounts of patient data during routine care, yet much remains underutilized due to limited research and analytical capacity. SHM established the Research and Training Unit (SHM R&T) in August 2023.
Objectives: To describe the establishment and early contributions of the SHM Research and Training Unit in promoting research capacity and evidence-based healthcare in Tanzania.
Methods: Institutional review of activities and outputs since August 2023 using records of research projects, collaborations, publications, and training initiatives.
Results: SHM R&T coordinated multiple projects including the multicenter META Trial, established collaborations with UCL, ISGlobal, UCD, and others, contributed to over 100 peer-reviewed publications, and trained over 500 individuals.
Conclusion: SHM R&T demonstrates how institutional investment in research and training can transform routine healthcare data into meaningful evidence. Similar platforms should be strengthened across Tanzania.
Strengthening Quality Research and Teaching in Health University through Academic Libraries: Experiences from MUHAS
Rehema Chande · Librarian · MUHAS
Co-authors: Neema Maluka
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality research and effective teaching are essential for advancing evidence-based healthcare education. Academic libraries support research productivity and information literacy, but many institutions in developing countries face challenges in accessing adequate electronic resources.
Objectives: To examine the contribution of the MUHAS Library in improving research quality and teaching excellence between 2022 and 2025.
Methods: Descriptive institutional review using library reports, repository statistics, bibliometric records, training reports, and plagiarism screening data. Eight key informants were interviewed.
Results: Journal guidance: 3,480 users; literature search assistance: 2,964 users; 186 training sessions for 4,720 participants. Over 630 researchers supported; 11,843 global citations received. Repository expanded from 1,840 to 3,207 items; annual publications increased from 690 to 851.
Conclusion: Academic libraries are strategic contributors to research quality, scholarly communication, and evidence-based teaching. Sustained investment in digital library infrastructure is essential for advancing healthcare education in Tanzania.
Improving Emergency Care in Tanzania: Scaling Capacity and Improving Systems through a Public-Private Partnership Model
Hussein Manji · Doctor · Aga Khan Hospital
Co-authors: Aleksandra Mizerska, Elishah Premji, Sisawo Konteh
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Emergency care gaps significantly contribute to preventable mortality in Tanzania. Baseline assessments in 2022 found fewer than 30% of providers had emergency training, triage delays of 20-30 minutes, and over 40% of essential equipment unavailable.
Objectives: The IMECT project aimed to strengthen emergency care by training providers, improving clinical systems, and deploying essential equipment across seven facilities through a scalable public-private partnership model.
Methods: Multi-component approach combining needs assessments, training, infrastructure, and system strengthening. Over 250 sessions delivered blended learning with simulation and bedside mentorship. Training-of-trainers model produced 160 instructors.
Results: 2,336 providers trained, exceeding the 1,500 target. Standardized triage systems doubled quality care. Facilities reported reduced emergency deaths, referrals, improved patient flow, and faster critical interventions.
Conclusion: IMECT achieved strong system-level impact, improving emergency care quality, clinical outcomes, and workforce confidence. The model is scalable with national relevance and potential for replication in resource-limited settings.
Effect of Learning Infrastructures on Nursing Students' Academic Performance at Dodoma Institute of Health and Allied Sciences
Mwahija Amrani · Nurse · Dodoma Regional Referral Hospital
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Learning infrastructures are critical determinants of academic performance in health training institutions. Tanzania's CQI Guideline aimed to strengthen teaching quality through improved infrastructures and continuous monitoring.
Objectives: To evaluate the effect of learning infrastructures on nursing students' academic performance at DIHAS, comparing pre-CQI (2019) and post-CQI (2024) cohorts.
Methods: Quasi-experimental study with 172 nursing students. Structured questionnaires and multiple linear regression with robust standard errors in STATA to determine associations between infrastructures and GPA.
Results: ICT facilities (β=0.42), skills laboratories (β=0.31), and library resources (β=0.27) significantly improved academic performance. Model explained 78.1% of variance in academic performance (F=59.68, p<0.001).
Conclusion: Learning infrastructures have a significant positive effect on nursing students' academic performance. Sustained investment in ICT, practical skills laboratories, and libraries within health training institutions is recommended.
Perception and Challenges of using Artificial Intelligence in Post Graduate Medical Education in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Dr. Nahida Walli · Paediatrician · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Mariam Noorani, Aliakbar Zehri, Hajaj Salum, Hanifa Mbithe, Daudi Gidion, Elisamia Ngowi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: AI is increasingly integrated into healthcare and medical education, offering opportunities to improve learning efficiency and educational delivery. Concerns remain regarding its ethical use, reliability, and impact on clinical reasoning, particularly in LMICs where evidence is limited.
Objectives: To explore perceptions and challenges related to the use of AI in postgraduate medical education among residents and faculty members in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.
Methods: Descriptive qualitative study across three universities. Four Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) with 27 residents and fifteen Key Informant Interviews (KIIs) with 13 faculty members. Interviews audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using content analysis with MAXQDA version 24.
Results: Five major themes emerged: (1) AI as a supportive learning tool, (2) concerns regarding overreliance and reduced clinical reasoning, (3) concerns about accuracy and contextual relevance of AI-generated information, (4) barriers to effective AI use within the local context, and (5) the need for structured institutional support.
Conclusion: AI is perceived as a valuable supportive tool in postgraduate medical education in Tanzania. Structured training, institutional policies, and improved access to reliable AI platforms are essential for responsible integration of AI into medical education.
Role of Preimplantation Genetic Testing in Enhancing IVF Outcomes: A Focus on Implantation and Pregnancy Rates
Muralidhar Babu · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Co-authors: Dr. Madhav Hirani, Dr. Sheila Karim Ramadhani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) identifies chromosomally normal embryos prior to transfer. Aneuploidy is a major factor contributing to implantation failure and miscarriages, especially in advanced maternal age patients.
Objectives: To assess the impact of PGT on implantation rate and clinical pregnancy outcomes in IVF cycles in a Tanzanian clinical setting, including local and cross-border patient populations.
Methods: Retrospective study at Nulife Advanced Fertility Center. 242 IVF cycles with PGT analyzed. Embryos cultured to blastocyst, biopsied on Day 5/6, analyzed by Next-Generation Sequencing. Only euploid embryos transferred.
Results: 680 embryos biopsied, 55% (374) euploid. Overall implantation and clinical pregnancy rate: 67%. Low early pregnancy loss rate of 6% for both local and international patients.
Conclusion: PGT greatly improved implantation and pregnancy outcomes. PGT-A is recommended as part of routine IVF care, especially for women with advanced maternal age or previous implantation failures.

Rehema Chande · Librarian · MUHAS
Improving Emergency Care in Tanzania: Scaling Capacity and Improving Systems through a Public–Private Partnership Model
Hussein Manji · Doctor · Aga Khan Hospital
Effect of Learning Infrastructures on Nursing Students' Academic Performance at Dodoma Institute of Health and Allied Sciences
Mwahija Amrani · Nurse · Dodoma Regional Referral Hospital
Perception and Challenges of using Artificial Intelligence in Post Graduate Medical Education in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Dr. Nahida Walli · Paediatrician · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Mariam Noorani, Aliakbar Zehri, Hajaj Salum, Hanifa Mbithe, Daudi Gidion, Elisamia Ngowi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: AI is increasingly integrated into healthcare and medical education, offering opportunities to improve learning efficiency and educational delivery. Concerns remain regarding its ethical use, reliability, and impact on clinical reasoning, particularly in LMICs where evidence is limited.
Objectives: To explore perceptions and challenges related to the use of AI in postgraduate medical education among residents and faculty members in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.
Methods: Descriptive qualitative study across three universities. Four Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) with 27 residents and fifteen Key Informant Interviews (KIIs) with 13 faculty members. Interviews audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using content analysis with MAXQDA version 24.
Results: Five major themes emerged: (1) AI as a supportive learning tool, (2) concerns regarding overreliance and reduced clinical reasoning, (3) concerns about accuracy and contextual relevance of AI-generated information, (4) barriers to effective AI use within the local context, and (5) the need for structured institutional support.
Conclusion: AI is perceived as a valuable supportive tool in postgraduate medical education in Tanzania. Structured training, institutional policies, and improved access to reliable AI platforms are essential for responsible integration of AI into medical education.
Role of Preimplantation Genetic Testing in Enhancing IVF Outcomes: A Focus on Implantation and Pregnancy Rates
Muralidhar Babu · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Poster Presentations
Day 2 · 5 June 2026
Feasibility, Acceptability and Effectiveness of Simulated Laughter Therapy on Depression among MDR-TB Patients in Northern Tanzania
Neema Makupa · Nurse · KCMC
Co-authors: Michael Burke
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: MDR-TB patients experience significant psychological distress, particularly depression, due to prolonged hospitalization and lengthy treatment. Simulated laughter therapy is a non-pharmacological intervention shown to improve psychological well-being.
Objectives: To assess the feasibility, acceptability and effectiveness of simulated laughter therapy on depression among MDR-TB patients hospitalized at Kibong'oto Infectious Disease Hospital.
Methods: Pre and post quasi-experimental design. 20 MDR-TB patients recruited consecutively. Questionnaires at recruitment, week 4, and week 8. Participants received simulated laughter therapy twice weekly for 8 consecutive weeks.
Results: Findings will inform the design of a larger scale trial and contribute to integrating mental health support into MDR-TB care.
Conclusion: The mental health challenges of long-term hospitalised MDR-TB patients are burdensome. Non-pharmacological interventions can contribute to reducing this burden.
Disseminated Iatrogenic Upper Gastrointestinal Kaposi Sarcoma Following Prolonged Steroid Use in a Patient with Crohn's Disease: A Case Report
Glory Makupa · Doctor · KCMC
Co-authors: Furaha Serventi, Leila Mwakipunda, Godwin Nnko, Edwin Liwa, Salum Lidenge, and others
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Kaposi sarcoma is a malignant vascular neoplasm caused by human herpesvirus-8, mostly seen in immunocompromised individuals. Iatrogenic KS may develop in patients on immunosuppressive medication. Intestinal KS in inflammatory bowel disease patients is uncommon.
Objectives: To describe the clinical presentation, management, and outcome of steroid-induced disseminated upper gastrointestinal Kaposi sarcoma in a patient with Crohn's disease.
Methods: Case report of a 39-year-old African male with Crohn's disease on long-term corticosteroid treatment. Clinical evaluation, investigations, multidisciplinary review, and treatment with biologic therapy and systemic chemotherapy.
Results: Patient developed extensive KS while on infliximab; started on paclitaxel chemotherapy. Completed 6 cycles with good tolerance and marked clinical response. Disease-free at 6-month follow-up with improved gastrointestinal symptoms.
Conclusion: Maintain a high index of suspicion for Kaposi sarcoma in patients on chronic immunosuppressive therapy presenting with unusual skin or mucosal lesions.
Kawasaki Disease in African Children: A Case Series
Mayila Mbuki · Doctor · AKU
Co-authors: Dr Deogratius Nkya
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute pediatric vasculitis and a leading cause of acquired heart disease in children. Although traditionally associated with Asian populations, it is increasingly recognized in Sub-Saharan Africa where it remains underdiagnosed.
Objectives: To report three cases of Kawasaki disease in African pediatric patients and highlight diagnostic challenges and management outcomes.
Methods: Case series of three African pediatric patients (two aged 2 years, one aged 6 years) presenting with prolonged fever unresponsive to antibiotics and classical mucocutaneous features.
Results: All patients received IVIG and high-dose aspirin with rapid fever resolution. One patient developed coronary artery ectasia on echocardiography. All had favourable outcomes at follow-up.
Conclusion: KD remains underdiagnosed in African pediatric populations. Persistent fever >5 days with mucocutaneous features should raise suspicion. Early IVIG treatment prevents cardiovascular complications.
Acute Postpartum Respiratory Distress With Right Ventricular Dysfunction Complicating Into Postpartum Cardiomyopathy: A Case of Suspected Atypical Amniotic Fluid Embolism
Aniceth Muchunguzi · Doctor · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is a devastating obstetric emergency typically characterized by sudden cardiopulmonary collapse. It is a diagnosis of exclusion, with atypical presentations overlapping with peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) and pulmonary edema.
Objectives: To describe a postpartum case presenting with acute respiratory distress and right ventricular dysfunction, illustrating the diagnostic complexity of postpartum cardiopulmonary collapse.
Methods: Case report: CT pulmonary angiography excluded pulmonary embolism; echocardiography revealed right heart strain with biventricular dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension. Managed with non-invasive ventilation, pulmonary vasodilators, and diuretics.
Results: Patient improved with resolution of respiratory distress. Discharged after 4 days total. At 10 weeks postpartum, repeat echocardiography showed persistent LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF 36%), fulfilling criteria for peripartum cardiomyopathy.
Conclusion: Highlights diagnostic complexity of postpartum cardiopulmonary collapse and the need for echocardiography-guided management and vigilant surveillance following unexplained postpartum respiratory failure.
Assessment of Adherence to Self-Monitoring of Blood Glucose in Children and Adolescents with Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus at Mwananyamala Regional Referral Hospital
Khadija Feruz · Intern · AKH
Co-authors: Dr. Leonard J. Malasa
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus requires regular self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) for effective management. Adherence to SMBG among young patients in Tanzania remains under-examined.
Objectives: To assess adherence to SMBG in children and adolescents with T1DM at Mwananyamala Regional Referral Hospital from September to November 2024.
Methods: Descriptive cross-sectional study of T1DM patients and caregivers. Data collected via questionnaires, analyzed using SPSS Version 23.
Results: 90.7% of 140 participants had access to glucometers, but only 50.7% adhered to recommended SMBG frequency of 3-4 times daily. Caregiver education and glucometer training influenced adherence.
Conclusion: Despite high glucometer access, adherence is suboptimal. Targeted training and caregiver support are needed to improve SMBG adherence in this population.
Knowledge, Attitudes and Practices Regarding Oral Malodor among Outpatients Attending Mbeya Zonal Referral Hospital, Tanzania
Shani Omari · Intern · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Oral malodor affects 22-50% of populations globally. In Tanzania, self-reported prevalence ranges from 44% in hospital settings to 72% among adolescents, yet localized data on knowledge, attitudes, and preventive practices remain scarce.
Objectives: To assess knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding oral malodor among outpatients at Mbeya Zonal Referral Hospital and identify factors associated with good preventive practices.
Methods: Hospital-based cross-sectional study of 204 outpatients (February-April 2026). Electronic questionnaires via KoboToolbox covering KAP. Binary logistic regression identified independent predictors of good practices.
Results: 89.2% demonstrated poor knowledge. 92.2% expressed high concern about psychosocial impact. Only 11.3% engaged in good practices. Knowledge (OR=1.048, p=0.001) and education level (OR=3.136, p<0.001) independently predicted good practices.
Conclusion: Severe knowledge-attitude-practice gap documented. Interventions must combine knowledge translation with removal of structural barriers. Integrating oral health education into primary care and reducing cost barriers are recommended.
Exploring Experiences of Online Gender-Based Violence among Female Undergraduate University Students in Dar es Salaam
Lorraine David · Intern · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Online gender-based violence (OGBV) is a growing threat among female university students. It includes cyberstalking, cyber harassment, and involuntary posting of indecent content, with serious psychological and academic impacts. Limited research exists in Tanzania.
Objectives: To explore experiences of OGBV among female undergraduate students, focusing on common forms, contributing factors, coping mechanisms, and institutional responses.
Methods: Exploratory qualitative study at MUHAS and KIUT. 16 Key Informant Interviews, two Focus Group Discussions, and policy document review. Thematic analysis of data.
Results: Five forms of OGBV identified: cyberbullying, image-based sexual violence, unwanted sexual messages, sexist hate speech, and doxing. Institutional support systems were variably known, underused, and perceived as ineffective.
Conclusion: OGBV is a neglected but far-reaching threat to Tanzanian female students. Digital safety training, student-centered policies, awareness campaigns, and enhanced institutional protection mechanisms are urgently needed.
